metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the BRE-alternation grep in the mutation-check AC with a portable form: `grep -En 'defect-seeded|re-introduce' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` (note `-E` for ERE, no backslash before `|`).
findings.finding: The proving command `grep -n 'defect-seeded\|re-introduce' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` uses BRE alternation (`\|`), which is a GNU grep extension. On macOS with BSD grep, `\|` is treated as a literal pipe character, so the pattern matches nothing and the command exits 1 — silently invalidating the AC on any macOS dev machine without producing a useful error.
findings.location: AC: 'rebar-debug Step 5's mutation check is defect-seeded'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the symlink-detection grep to the mode column: replace `grep 120000` with `grep '^120000'` so only lines whose mode field is exactly 120000 match.
findings.finding: The symlink-absence proving command `git ls-files -s examples/claude-skills | grep 120000` uses an unanchored pattern; any file whose path contains the string '120000' (however unlikely) would produce a false positive, causing the AC to report a symlink where none exists.
findings.location: AC: 'For each of rebar-debug, rebar-implement, rebar-brainstorm: … byte-identical … `git ls-files -s examples/claude-skills | grep 120000` is empty'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the style AC's 'six touched prompt files' list: either explicitly enumerate the six files by name (to make clear README.md is included for completeness, not as a prompt-style target) or separate README.md from the prompt-style review scope consistent with the body's definition of 'prompt text addressing the LLM user'.
findings.finding: The style AC names 'all six touched prompt files' and requires a per-file style affirmation, but examples/claude-skills/README.md — one of the six files — is developer-facing documentation, not LLM-user-addressed prompt text. The plan body defines the prompt style rule as applying to 'All added/changed prompt text [that] addresses the LLM user of the skill', which README.md does not. An implementer satisfying this AC may either incorrectly apply LLM-prompt style standards to README.md or be unsure whether README.md should pass or fail the positive-first phrasing check.
findings.location: AC: Style check (two parts) — 'a comment on this ticket lists all six touched prompt files with a per-file affirmation of positive-first phrasing'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'all six touched prompt files' with an explicit enumeration: shared/test-design.md, rebar-debug/SKILL.md, rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md, rebar-implement/SKILL.md, rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md — and note that README.md is excluded from the prompt-style check (it is developer documentation). Adjust the count to 'five prompt files' accordingly.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact post-edit wording for `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md`'s Phase 2 (Repair) bullet — e.g. append '(for LLM-surface evals, quantification follows `test-design.md`: multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control)' — so the executor has a concrete target and the single-sample description is unambiguously superseded rather than left in conflict.
findings.finding: The plan instructs that llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md's Phase 2 (Repair) bullet be 'QUALIFIED (not deleted or fully rephrased)' by adding a citation to test-design.md, but specifies no target wording for the edited bullet. The current bullet reads 'RED = an eval/behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction' (single-sample). After the edit, that single-sample definition coexists with a citation pointing to test-design.md's multi-sample, predeclared-threshold standard. An LLM executor has no specified reconciliation: should the bullet now read 'RED = an eval/behavioural assertion (quantification per test-design.md)' or something else? The AC only checks that 'test-design.md' appears in the bullet's awk window — it does not verify that the single-sample definition is harmonized with the multi-sample standard.
findings.location: Plan: 'Skill integration' → rebar-debug section, llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md bullet; AC: '`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md`'s Phase 2 (Repair) bullet is QUALIFIED'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md bullet: 'The updated sentence reads: "Phase 2 (Repair) — RED = an eval/behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction; for LLM-surface evals, quantification follows `test-design.md` (multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control); fix under KERNEL + affirmative-framing; GREEN = the assertion passes and the original report no longer reproduces."'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the existing heading convention for Phase 4a/4b/4c in rebar-implement/SKILL.md (e.g. `### 4a`, `## Phase 4a`) and align the awk range pattern in the AC to match it exactly, so the proving command is unambiguously correct.
findings.finding: The rebar-implement AC proving command `awk '/^### 4a/,/^### 4c/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-implement/SKILL.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'` silently produces empty output — and thus a false AC failure — if the existing SKILL.md uses a different heading level or format (e.g. `## Phase 4a`, `#### 4a`, `**4a**`). The plan states 'Phase 4a gains an explicit instruction sentence' but does not declare the heading convention the existing file uses, leaving the executor unable to predict whether the awk range will match.
findings.location: AC: 'rebar-implement Phase 4a/4b cite the shared file inside those sections'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the inaccurate 'mirrors the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' framing with an accurate description: the new pytest test is a static byte-identity comparison auto-collected by `make test`'s existing `pytest -m 'not integration and not external'` sweep, requiring no CI workflow YAML changes (unlike the CI-script gates for public-types and env-vars, which do require YAML entries enforced by test_ci_workflow_parity.py).
findings.finding: The plan describes the proposed `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` as 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)', but probing the repo refutes this analogy: `public-types` and `env-vars` are CI-script drift gates that require entries in BOTH `test.yml` AND `gerrit-verify.yaml` (enforced by `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`); `prompt-index` uses a regenerate-and-diff pytest test, not static copy-equality. The proposed pattern (byte-identity between static file copies) matches none of these three and requires no workflow-YAML changes. An implementer following the stated analogy could add unnecessary CI workflow steps that break `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`, or misunderstand whether workflow registration is required.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Reference mechanism' section: 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the parenthetical from '(prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' to a clearer statement: 'auto-collected by make test's existing pytest sweep; no workflow YAML registration needed (unlike CI-script gates such as public-types and env-vars).' This removes the false analogy and accurately describes what an implementer must do.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the style-check AC (or add a note in the authoring guidance) to detect inline multi-item 'never X, Y, Z' prohibition lists in test-design.md, not just '^(Bad|Wrong):' blocks and 'do NOT assert', so the file's own prohibitions comply with the positive-first framing rule it enforces.
findings.finding: The plan's inline bullet descriptions of test-design.md content use 'never X' elaborations that name prohibited patterns (e.g., 'never mock call counts, private helpers, intermediate payloads, or source text'; 'never timing-only sleeps as the primary mechanism'). The plan's own prompt-style constraint says 'never elaborate negative examples that prime the model toward the prohibited behavior' and the style-check AC only catches '^(Bad|Wrong):' blocks and 'do NOT assert'. An executor faithfully transcribing the plan's bullet wording into test-design.md's sections will produce a file with pink-elephant-priming elaborations that the AC's deterministic check does not catch.
findings.location: Plan: 'The shared file covers' → 'Trigger the mechanism' and 'Fault realism' bullets; AC: Style check deterministic grep (`! grep -nE '^(Bad|Wrong):'` and `! grep -n 'do NOT assert'`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the style-check AC: `! grep -nE 'never mock|never.*private|never timing' examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md` as a proxy, or add an authoring note: 'state each prohibition as a one-line directive (e.g., "assert the public consequence") without listing the prohibited alternatives.'

findings.finding: The Step 4 heading AC's proving command `grep -n '^## Step 4' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` matches ANY '## Step 4' line — it does NOT assert that 'RED oracle' appears in the heading. The prove narrative says 'prints a heading containing RED oracle' but the grep pattern has no such anchor, so the command passes even if the heading reads '## Step 4 – Design a test' (no 'RED oracle').
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'rebar-debug Step 4 is retitled to RED-oracle design. Prove: grep -n ...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the proving command with: `grep -n '^## Step 4.*RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`

findings.finding: AC13 (style check) names no deterministic proving command and is framed as a manual human review ('reviewed section-by-section against the pink-elephant rule stated above'). There is no repo artifact, CI check, or deterministic command that produces evidence on success, making it neither in-session-evaluable by an automated agent nor falsifiable by CI.

The llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 AC uses `grep -c 'test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md >= 1` but this only proves the string exists somewhere in the file — it does NOT prove the hit is inside the Phase 2 (Repair) bullet. The plan's requirement 'with the hit inside the Phase 2 bullet' has no machine-verifiable proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (Repair) bullet is QUALIFIED. Prove: grep -c ...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or augment the grep-c command with: `awk '/Phase 2.*Repair/,/Phase [3-9]/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-field activation rules for the contract card's conditional fields — specify the exact condition that triggers each of: negative control (input-class defects or contrast-case default), collateral invariants (stateful operations), recovery (atomicity/recoverability contract), and fault injection (crash/recovery tests) — so the LLM executor authoring test-design.md has a precise schema, not a single undifferentiated 'stateful/atomicity/concurrency' trigger.
findings.finding: The contract card's conditional fields ('negative control, collateral invariants, recovery, fault injection') are described as applying 'conditionally (stateful/atomicity/concurrency work)', but no precise trigger vocabulary or decision rule is given for when each conditional field activates. An LLM executor authoring the contract card section of test-design.md has no enumerated decision rules for 'when does collateral invariants apply vs. not' or 'what exactly qualifies as stateful work' — the field is referenced but its activation schema is undefined.
findings.location: Plan: 'The shared file covers' → test contract card bullet; AC bullet 1 (section-by-section read against the bullet list)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
